notes: Chariot finale cards. The bus fails in distinctive ways — out of fuel before the depot, a passenger went past their stop, the route deviated. The road keeps going through all of them. Cora has driven this road five hundred times. Tonight is one of them.

finales.title: OUT OF FUEL
finales.flavor: The bus is on the shoulder, two miles from the depot. The fuel gauge is below the line. The passengers are quiet. Cora reaches for the CB. The CB is just hum — channel 17 has gone empty at this hour, the way it sometimes does on the long quiet stretches. The cicadas outside the bus are very loud. She pulls the parking brake. She turns the headlights off and then turns them back on because somebody behind her in the dark might need them. The late-shift worker stands up at the back, comes to the front, sits in the seat next to her. They wait together for the next bus or the morning, whichever comes first.

finales.title: THE DRIVER DRIFTED
finales.flavor: Cora has been on the road for nine hours. The lane markers blur — first the way they always blur at this hour, then a different way. The bus drifts into the breakdown lane. She catches it. The bus catches it with her. She pulls onto the shoulder. The passengers are quiet because passengers always are after a drift. She turns the engine off. She gets out. She stands on the shoulder gravel and looks at the road. The road does not look back. She does not get back in for a long while. When she does, she drives the rest of the route at thirty-five and arrives at the depot eleven minutes late. The relief driver is asleep on a bench. Nobody asks why she's late.

finales.title: THE PASSENGER WENT PAST
finales.flavor: Three passengers rode past the stop they paid for. The late-shift worker is one of them — they fell asleep against the cold window, the way they sometimes do after a fourteen-hour shift. The lost traveler is one of them; the stop they needed went by while Cora was watching the kid in the back. The third one Cora doesn't recognize. The next-town depot is awake. The night-clerk there will help them figure it out. They'll get home or to wherever home was supposed to be tonight. Cora marks the night in the route-book at the depot. She does not write down which three.

finales.title: THE ROUTE RAN LONG
finales.flavor: Five AM. The bus is at the destination depot. The next driver is waiting in the lot with a thermos and the same expression he has every morning. The passengers all got off at their stops, more or less — the kid running away got off at a stop that wasn't theirs and walked the rest, which is how those nights go. The trip was the trip. The route ran longer than it should have because Cora pulled over once, on the long stretch, and sat for ten minutes with the engine off because the dashboard clock had gotten louder than she wanted it to be. The next driver doesn't ask. He never asks.

post_loss_behavior.note: Cora's losses carry a little residual fuel forward. The bus remembers. The bus has been remembering since 1991, which is when the seat took her shape.
